concepts.lesson: India is located entirely in the Northern Hemisphere. The mainland lies between latitudes 8°4'N and 37°6'N and longitudes 68°7'E and 97°25'E.
concepts.real_example: On a world map, India is located in South Asia on the Asian continent.
key_points: India lies in the Northern Hemisphere

concepts.lesson: The Tropic of Cancer (23°30'N) divides India into tropical and subtropical regions. It passes almost through the middle of the country.
concepts.real_example: States like Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh and West Bengal are crossed by the Tropic of Cancer.
key_points: Divides India into two climatic zones
Passes through the middle of India

concepts.lesson: India has a land area of about 3.28 million square kilometres and is the seventh largest country in the world.
concepts.real_example: Countries like Russia, Canada and China are larger than India.

concepts.lesson: India is bounded by the Himalayas in the north and surrounded by the Arabian Sea in the west, the Bay of Bengal in the east and the Indian Ocean in the south.
concepts.real_example: The Himalayas form a natural barrier between India and Central Asia.
key_points: Bay of Bengal in the east

concepts.lesson: India uses the longitude 82°30'E as the Standard Meridian for Indian Standard Time to maintain uniform time across the country.
concepts.real_example: Mirzapur in Uttar Pradesh lies close to the standard meridian used for IST.
key_points: Ensures uniform time across the country

concepts.lesson: India has a central location in Asia between East and West. This location helped India maintain trade and cultural relations with many regions.
concepts.real_example: Ancient trade routes connected India with Europe, Africa and East Asia.

concepts.lesson: India's location on major trade routes helped the spread of ideas, culture and goods between different civilizations.
concepts.real_example: Indian numerals and the decimal system spread to other parts of the world.

concepts.lesson: India shares land boundaries with Pakistan, Afghanistan, China, Nepal, Bhutan, Bangladesh and Myanmar.
concepts.real_example: Nepal and Bhutan lie to the north of India.
key_points: Pakistan and Afghanistan in the northwest
China, Nepal and Bhutan in the north
Bangladesh and Myanmar in the east
Sri Lanka and Maldives are island neighbours

concepts.lesson: India has two main island groups: Lakshadweep in the Arabian Sea and Andaman & Nicobar Islands in the Bay of Bengal.
concepts.real_example: Lakshadweep islands are located southwest of the Indian mainland.
key_points: Andaman & Nicobar in Bay of Bengal

concepts.lesson: India’s central location in the Indian Ocean helped it become an important centre for trade, cultural exchange and political influence in ancient and modern times.
concepts.real_example: Sea routes through the Indian Ocean connected Europe, Africa and East Asia through India.
